summary: Intel is sitting on thousands of unsellable AI accelerators (Gaudi 2 and Data Center GPU Max 1450) due to fundamental software and hardware design problems. The Gaudi architecture is nearly impossible to program because its compute units aren't controllable by programmable cores, making it only useful for hand-coded workloads. The Max 1450 GPUs require exotic infrastructure to even plug in, and Intel's negotiations to sell them were paralyzed by committee leadership that couldn't make decisions. Hotz argues that Intel's AI roadmap is effectively dead, with multiple successor chips cancelled and no credible path forward, contrasting this with AMD's slow-but-steady progress under decisive leadership.
key_points: Intel has 15,000 Gaudi 2 cards and 5,100 Max 1450 GPUs sitting in inventory with no buyers, largely due to unusable software
Gaudi's architecture is fundamentally flawed for training - the MMEs are controlled by queues rather than programmable cores, making it nearly impossible to deviate from hand-coded paths
The Max 1450 requires OAM sockets, 48V power, and 600W cooling solutions that barely exist outside supercomputers, making deployment impractical
Intel rejected tiny corp's $200/GPU offer despite having no other buyers, demonstrating organizational paralysis where no individual has power to make deals
Intel's AI roadmap is a graveyard of cancellations (Ponte Vecchio successor, Rialto Bridge, Falcon Shores), leaving 'Jaguar Shores' with zero credibility
money_quotes: Intel is sitting on a huge amount of card inventory they can't move, largely because of bad software.
The architecture is kind of tragic, and that's likely why they didn't open source it.
This will never be acceptable for a training chip.
Before you say you'd pay more, ask yourself, do you really want to deal with the software?
I wasn't able to find an individual with the power to make a deal, never mind actually make a deal. It's unclear any individual in the company has that power.

There's no point in investing in the platform if there's not going to be a next generation, and therefore nobody can justify the cost of developing software, therefore there won't be software, therefore they aren't worth plugging in.
No point even bothering to listen to their roadmaps anymore. They have squandered all their credibility.
The problem with this is there's nobody who can say yes, just many people who can say no.
key_insight: Intel's AI hardware isn't failing because of bad chips alone, but because committee-style leadership creates organizational paralysis where nobody can say yes to deals, investments, or strategic pivots - a death spiral for a company that needs radical change to compete.
